Press enter or click to view image in full size

We had done the hard part. Or so I thought.

6 min read
Just now

--

The Room That Felt Aligned
The architecture meeting had been on the calendar for three weeks. Six engineers, two product managers, one engineering director. The agenda was clear: decide the data storage strategy for the new analytics platform before the build started. We had done the preparation. Two engineers had researched the options. A document had circulated. Everyone had read it, or at least everyone had said they had read it.
The meeting opened with a summary of the two approaches. A relational model with PostgreSQL, normalized schema, flexible querying. A columnar approach with a managed data warehouse, optimized for the aggregation queries the analytics layer would need to run.
Fifteen minutes in, the director said the columnar approach sounded right for analytics workloads and asked if there were objections. There were no objections. We moved on.
I wrote up the decision in the shared document that afternoon. Columnar storage. Managed warehouse. Build starts Monday.
I felt good about the process. We had a document. We had a meeting. We had a decision.
What we did not have was alignment. Those are not the same thing.
What Silence in a Meeting Actually Means
Three weeks into the build, one of the engineers who had been in the room came to me with a concern she had not raised in the meeting.
Her name was Fatima. She was the engineer most familiar with our existing data pipeline. She said the columnar approach assumed a data ingestion pattern that our pipeline did not currently support and would require a significant rewrite of the ingestion layer before the analytics platform could receive data in the format the warehouse expected.
I asked her why she had not raised this in the meeting.
She said she had not been certain enough at the time. The director had asked for objections and she had not wanted to slow the room down with a concern she could not fully articulate in the moment. By the time she had thought it through clearly enough to be confident it was a real problem, the meeting was over and the decision was documented and it felt too late to reopen it.
It was not too late at three weeks. It was expensive at three weeks. It would have been free at the meeting.
The silence in the room had not been agreement. It had been uncertainty that did not have enough space to become a question before the decision closed.
The Cost That Accumulated Quietly
The ingestion layer rewrite took eleven weeks. It had not been in the project plan. It had not been in the timeline the director had approved. It had not been in any estimate anyone had produced before the build started because nobody had known it was necessary until Fatima had three weeks to think through the implications of a decision that had been made in fifteen minutes.
While the ingestion layer was being rewritten, the analytics platform build continued in parallel on the assumption that the rewrite would complete on schedule. It did not complete on schedule because rewriting a production ingestion layer while keeping it operational for the existing consumers it served is not an eleven-week job when you do it cleanly. It is a seventeen-week job when you do it without breaking the things that depend on it.
The analytics platform launched six months late. The team that had been waiting for it had built manual workarounds in the interim that became difficult to decommission because the workarounds had accumulated their own dependencies by the time the platform was ready.
One concern. Not raised in the meeting. Fifteen minutes of silence that cost six months of schedule and seventeen weeks of unplanned engineering work on a system that had been running correctly before we decided to rewrite it.
What the Meeting Structure Had Made Impossible
After the launch I went back and looked at how the meeting had been structured. The director had summarized the options, expressed a preference, and asked for objections. That structure is extremely common. It is also extremely effective at producing the appearance of alignment while making it structurally difficult to surface genuine concerns.
When a senior person in the room expresses a preference and asks for objections, several things happen simultaneously that are not visible in the meeting itself. Engineers who have partial concerns but cannot fully articulate them in the moment stay silent because raising a half-formed concern in front of a director feels riskier than staying quiet. Engineers who disagree but are junior to the person who expressed the preference calculate whether the disagreement is worth the social cost before deciding whether to speak. Engineers who have not yet thought through the full implications of the decision cannot raise concerns about implications they have not yet reached.
The meeting had lasted fifteen minutes because the structure had optimized for speed and the room had responded accordingly. The speed had felt like efficiency. It was the absence of the conversation that would have cost fifteen minutes and saved six months.
What I Changed About How Decisions Get Made
The change I made after that project was not about the length of meetings or the number of people in them. It was about what the meeting was for.
The architecture meeting I had run was a decision meeting. Its purpose was to produce a documented decision by the end of the time block. That purpose had shaped every dynamic in the room, including Fatima’s silence, because in a decision meeting an unresolved concern feels like an obstacle to the goal rather than a contribution to it.
The meetings I run now for significant architecture decisions have a different stated purpose. The purpose is to surface what we do not yet know, not to produce a decision. The decision comes after, once the room has had enough space to generate the concerns that a well-structured meeting reliably suppresses.
That change is small and uncomfortable in the specific way that slower processes are uncomfortable when the calendar says the sprint starts Monday. It is also the change that would have cost fifteen minutes in the meeting and saved six months on the other side of it.
The Question That Should Have Been Asked
The question the director asked was: are there objections.
The question that would have changed the outcome was: what are we assuming is true that we have not verified.
Those two questions produce completely different responses from the same room. The first invites people to defend a concern against a decision that is already leaning one direction. The second invites people to surface uncertainty before the direction is set.
Fatima’s concern about the ingestion layer was not an objection to the columnar approach. It was an unverified assumption about our pipeline’s compatibility with the approach. In an objection frame, it did not fit. In an assumption frame, it was exactly what the question was asking for.
The architecture decision we made was not wrong. A columnar approach was the right call for the analytics workload. What was wrong was the assumption we had not named, which was that our existing pipeline could feed the warehouse without significant rework. That assumption had been wrong. Nobody had checked it. The question that would have surfaced it had not been asked.
The Framework That Prevents the Six-Month Discovery
The architecture decision process that surfaces unverified assumptions before the build starts rather than eleven weeks into it, the meeting structures that make it safe for Fatima to raise a half-formed concern before it becomes a six-month delay, the constraint questions that separate what the room knows from what the room is assuming, and the decision documentation format that distinguishes verified decisions from assumed dependencies that is inside The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook.
Because the architecture decisions that cost the most are almost never the ones where the room chose the wrong technology. They are the ones where the room chose the right technology against an assumption nobody had named, and the assumption was wrong, and Fatima knew it three weeks later but had not had enough space to know it fifteen minutes earlier when it would have been free.
The longer architecture decision breakdowns go out on Substack.
